// about

Who I Am

I'm Raphael, a cybersecurity practitioner focused on Blue Team operations and SOC analysis. I'm actively building practical, lab-based skills in threat detection, network analysis, log monitoring, and incident response.

My approach is project-driven and public — every lab I complete gets documented and pushed to GitHub. I believe in learning by doing, and in building a portfolio that shows real work, not just certifications.

I'm pursuing the TryHackMe SOC Level 1 path alongside a structured 10-project Blue Team lab roadmap, working with tools used by defenders in the real world.

// projects

Lab Projects

PROJECT_01
Home Cybersecurity Lab — Ubuntu + VirtualBox
Set up a personal cybersecurity lab environment using Ubuntu Linux on VirtualBox. Configured the VM for network analysis, performed Nmap reconnaissance scans, and captured live network traffic with Wireshark. Fully documented with screenshots and methodology notes.
Lesson learned: Building the lab environment itself — networking, VM resource allocation, getting interfaces talking to each other — took longer than the actual scanning. It taught me that infrastructure setup is its own skill, separate from the security tooling on top of it.
// screenshots
Ubuntu VirtualBox Nmap Wireshark Linux
View on GitHub →
PROJECT_02
Docker Vulnerability Container — ThousandEyes CVE Lab
Built a Dockerized vulnerability research environment targeting a ThousandEyes-related CVE. Implemented a mock API approach to simulate the platform due to corporate email restrictions on the live environment. Project includes a complete README, methodology, and screenshots demonstrating findings.
Lesson learned: When I hit the corporate email wall blocking direct access, I had to decide between giving up or simulating the environment. Building the mock API taught me that real-world constraints (legal, access, corporate policy) are part of the job — and that adapting the approach instead of abandoning the research is itself a useful skill.
// screenshots
Docker CVE Research API Mocking Vulnerability Analysis
View on GitHub →
PROJECT_03
Splunk Alert Triage & Investigation Lab
Built a Splunk-based alert investigation workflow simulating a SOC analyst environment. Ingested sample security logs, wrote SPL queries to surface suspicious activity, triaged alerts by severity, identified false positives, and documented escalation decisions — practising real SOC Tier 1 workflows end to end.
Lesson learned: My first SPL queries were too broad and buried real findings under noise. Tightening the query logic and learning to distinguish a true positive from a false positive by context, not just by the alert rule firing, was the actual skill this project taught me — not the syntax.
// screenshots
Splunk SPL Alert Triage Log Analysis SOC Workflows
View on GitHub →
PROJECT_04
Splunk SOC Security Monitoring Dashboard
Built a full SOC security monitoring dashboard in Splunk Enterprise with 4 interactive panels — Alert Severity Distribution, Event Type Breakdown, Top Attacker IPs, and a Critical Alerts Table. Visualised threat data from 59 security events to identify patterns, attacker behaviour, and critical incidents at a glance.
Lesson learned: My first dashboard draft showed every field I could think of, which made it harder to read, not easier. Cutting it down to the 4 panels that actually answer "what needs attention right now" taught me that good dashboard design is about subtraction, not addition.
// screenshots
Splunk Dashboard Data Visualization SOC Monitoring Threat Analysis
View on GitHub →
PROJECT_05
Home SOC Lab — Attack Simulation & Intrusion Detection
Built a full home SOC lab using Kali Linux as attacker and Ubuntu as defender on VirtualBox. Performed network reconnaissance with Nmap, captured attack traffic with Wireshark, and deployed Snort IDS with 3 custom detection rules. Successfully detected ICMP ping sweeps, port scans, and SSH connection attempts in real time.
// screenshots
Kali Linux Ubuntu Nmap Wireshark Snort IDS VirtualBox

Problem: Needed hands-on proof I could detect live network attacks, not just read about IDS theory.

Approach: Built a two-VM range — Kali as attacker, Ubuntu as defender — on VirtualBox. Ran reconnaissance (Nmap scans, ICMP sweeps) from Kali while monitoring traffic on Ubuntu with Wireshark, then wrote and deployed 3 custom Snort rules to catch the specific attack patterns.

Tools: Kali Linux, Ubuntu, VirtualBox, Nmap, Wireshark, Snort IDS.

Result: All 3 custom rules fired correctly in real time — successfully detecting ICMP ping sweeps, port scans, and SSH connection attempts. Documented full rule syntax and detection logic in the repo README.

Lesson learned: My first rule attempts were too generic and fired on legitimate traffic. Narrowing the rule conditions until alerts only fired on actual attack patterns taught me that tuning detection rules is as much about reducing false positives as it is about catching the real thing.

View on GitHub →
PROJECT_06
Phishing Email Analysis Lab
Analysed a realistic phishing email sample using manual header inspection, SPF/DKIM/DMARC verification, malicious link analysis, and social engineering detection. Identified typosquatting, Russian-hosted phishing infrastructure, forged sender headers, and correlated the sender IP with a known threat actor from a previous Splunk investigation — demonstrating real Threat Intelligence Correlation.
Lesson learned: The header analysis alone wasn't convincing — what made the case solid was correlating the sender IP back to a prior Splunk investigation. It taught me that phishing analysis is stronger when treated as connected intelligence work, not an isolated one-off email review.
// screenshots
Email Forensics Header Analysis SPF/DKIM/DMARC Threat Intel Social Engineering IOC Correlation
View on GitHub →
PROJECT_07
IDS Lab — Snort vs Suricata Comparison
Built a dual-IDS home SOC lab comparing Snort and Suricata side by side, using Kali Linux as the attacker and Ubuntu 24.04 as the monitoring host on a host-only VirtualBox network. Wrote matching custom rule sets for both engines and validated detection of ICMP ping sweeps, SYN port scans, and SSH connection attempts in real time — then documented a feature-by-feature comparison of threading, alert output, and performance between the two.
// screenshots
Snort Suricata Kali Linux Ubuntu Nmap VirtualBox

Problem: Wanted to understand the practical tradeoffs between two industry-standard IDS engines instead of just picking one — what SOC teams actually weigh when choosing a detection stack.

Approach: Deployed Kali (192.168.56.101) as attacker and Ubuntu 24.04 (192.168.56.102) as defender on a host-only VirtualBox network. Wrote parallel custom rule sets for Snort 2.9.20 and Suricata 7.0.3 targeting the same three attack patterns, then ran identical Nmap-based attacks against both engines to compare detection and output.

Tools: Snort 2.9.20, Suricata 7.0.3, Kali Linux, Ubuntu 24.04, VirtualBox, Nmap.

Result: Both engines correctly fired on ICMP ping sweeps, SYN scans, and SSH connection attempts using custom rules. Documented a feature comparison covering threading (single vs multi-threaded), alert output format (console vs JSON/eve.json), and performance characteristics for high vs low traffic environments.

Lesson learned: Writing the "same" rule twice for two different engines showed me that detection logic isn't actually portable — syntax, defaults, and matching behavior differ enough that a rule tuned for one engine needs real rework, not just translation, to behave the same way on the other.

View on GitHub →
PROJECT_08
Firewall Log Analysis & Threat Detection Lab
Built a Blue Team SOC lab analyzing pfSense firewall logs in Splunk Enterprise to detect real attack patterns. Ingested raw firewall logs into Splunk, wrote SPL queries to surface threats, and produced a full incident report — successfully identifying a port scan, an SSH brute force attempt, and Metasploit C2 traffic on port 4444.
Lesson learned: Spotting the Metasploit C2 traffic on port 4444 was the hardest part — it didn't look like an "attack" at a glance, just a connection on an unusual port. It taught me that firewall log analysis is less about scanning for obvious red flags and more about knowing which ports and behaviors are abnormal for the environment.
// screenshots
Splunk Enterprise pfSense SPL Incident Response SOC Monitoring

Problem: Needed to show I could go beyond IDS/host-based detection and work directly with firewall-level log data — the kind of raw telemetry most SOC Tier 1 roles deal with daily.

Approach: Ingested pfSense firewall logs into Splunk Enterprise, built SPL queries to isolate suspicious connection patterns, and cross-referenced flagged IPs and ports against known attack signatures (port scanning behavior, repeated auth failures, and known C2 ports).

Tools: Splunk Enterprise 10.4.0, pfSense firewall logs, SPL queries, Ubuntu Linux on VirtualBox.

Result: Detected and documented three distinct threats — a port scan from 192.168.1.105, an SSH brute force attempt from 185.220.101.45, and Metasploit C2 traffic on port 4444 — and wrote a full incident report covering each finding.

View on GitHub →

My Timeline

MARCH 2025
🚀 Started Cybersecurity Journey
Set up Ubuntu Linux on VirtualBox. Configured first home lab environment, ran initial Nmap scans and Wireshark captures. Opened GitHub account under raphsec and began documenting everything publicly.
APRIL 2025
🐋 Docker Vulnerability Research Lab
Built a Dockerized CVE research environment targeting a ThousandEyes vulnerability. Implemented mock API methodology, documented full findings with screenshots and pushed to GitHub.
MAY 2025
🎯 TryHackMe SOC Level 1 Path
Enrolled in TryHackMe's SOC Level 1 learning path. Began hands-on modules covering SIEM tools, network analysis, threat intelligence, and alert triage — building real SOC analyst skills.
MAY – JUNE 2025
📊 Splunk Alert Triage Lab
Deployed Splunk, ingested security logs, and built SPL queries to surface suspicious activity. Practised Tier 1 SOC workflows — alert triage, false positive identification, and escalation documentation.
MAY 2026
🏠 Home SOC Lab — Kali vs Ubuntu + Snort IDS
Built a full attacker vs defender lab using Kali Linux and Ubuntu on VirtualBox. Performed network reconnaissance with Nmap, captured traffic with Wireshark, deployed Snort IDS with 3 custom detection rules — all alerts fired successfully.
JUNE 2026
🎣 Phishing Email Analysis Lab
Analysed a realistic phishing email sample — identified typosquatting, SPF/DKIM/DMARC failures, Russian-hosted infrastructure, and social engineering tactics. Correlated the sender IP with a known threat actor from the Splunk investigation — demonstrating real Threat Intelligence Correlation.
JUNE 2026
🛡️ Snort vs Suricata IDS Comparison Lab
Built a dual-engine IDS lab comparing Snort and Suricata head-to-head — wrote matching custom rules for both, validated detections against ICMP sweeps, port scans, and SSH attempts, and documented a feature comparison covering threading, alert output, and performance.
JUNE 2026
🔥 Firewall Log Analysis & Threat Detection Lab
Built a SOC lab analyzing pfSense firewall logs in Splunk — detected and documented a port scan, an SSH brute force attempt, and Metasploit C2 traffic on port 4444, with a full incident report.
NOW → 2026
